# SCENIC+ for GRN Inference on multi-model \+ single cell data

Daniel C. Morgan, https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8326-6178, Olink Proteomics AB Uppsala SE

# Abstract

Single-cell RNA sequencing unveils intricate cellular diversity and the molecular underpinnings of cell identity at unprecedented resolution. SCENIC+ serves as a powerful computational framework that unifies transcription factor expression, motif enrichment, chromatin accessibility, and gene expression data to construct cell-type-specific gene regulatory networks (GRNs). Leveraging pycisTopic and pycisTarget, SCENIC+ delineates consensus enhancer regions from scATAC-seq data and infers transcription factor binding sites through motif enrichment analysis. By integrating these cis-regulatory insights with gene expression profiles, it generates enhancer-driven GRNs, offering a scalable and robust tool to decode the complex regulatory mechanisms shaping cellular function.

Keywords: Networks Inference, Single-Cell RNA-Seq, Multi-modal

# Introduction

Gene regulatory networks (GRNs)—graphs where nodes represent transcription factors (TFs), i.e. regulators, and target genes, while edges denote regulatory interactions—provide a systems-level framework for understanding cellular regulation. While experimental mapping of GRNs offers precise insights, its high cost and limited scalability restrict its ability to capture the full diversity of cell types and conditions. Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has revolutionized this landscape by profiling thousands of genes across millions of cells, exposing cell-to-cell variability and context-specific regulation. However, traditional GRN inference methods, often optimized for bulk data, falter at this scale, often relying on dimensionality reduction that risks oversimplifying the underlying biology.

SCENIC+ addresses these challenges as a comprehensive computational framework designed to infer robust, context-specific GRNs from single-cell data. It integrates multiple genomic layers—TF expression, motif enrichment, chromatin accessibility, and gene expression—to construct enhancer-driven regulatory networks. The workflow begins by identifying candidate enhancers with shared accessibility patterns from scATAC-seq data, followed by motif enrichment analysis to predict TF binding sites. It then computes separate importance scores for TF-to-gene (via GRNBoost2 regression on TF and target gene expression, signed by Pearson correlation) and region-to-gene (via GRNBoost2 on imputed region accessibility and target gene expression, signed by Spearman correlation) links. This process teases out regulatory interactions that embrace the non-deterministic nature of gene regulation, spotlighting dynamic and heterogeneous control through eRegulons. eRegulons are sets of genes regulated by a TF via specific enhancer regions—Topologically, an eRegulon is a triplet: TF → region → gene. The TF links to the regulatory region via motif enrichment (significant over-representation of the TF's binding motif in the open chromatin region). The region links to the target gene via GRNBoost2 importance scores (from regression predicting gene expression from region accessibility) signed by Spearman correlation between region accessibility and gene expression. TF-to-gene links (GRNBoost2 importance \+ Pearson correlation sign) further support the connection, yielding robust enhancer-driven triplets that integrate all three features.

Many single-cell multi-omics GRN tools exist, including but not limited to CellOracle for in silico perturbations[1](https://paperpile.com/c/T1DyUg/AeY9); Pando for motif-based regression [2](https://paperpile.com/c/T1DyUg/AeY9+DKVp); FigR for sparse correlations [3](https://paperpile.com/c/T1DyUg/AKsR); GRaNIE for linear models with enhancers [4](https://paperpile.com/c/T1DyUg/AKsR+KnkS). However, SCENIC+ claims to excels in predicting TF binding sites via clustered motif enrichment and differential motif analysis and target genes/regions (higher F1 scores, precision/recall against UniBind ChIP-seq/Enformer, stronger Hi-C correlations for region-gene links, better transcriptome prediction via regression). Definite limitations do exist and include computational intensity (especially motif steps and large datasets), reliance on high-quality multi-omics integration, potential stochasticity from underlying algorithms, and false positives from motif-based inference without direct binding data. Successful inference requires paired or unpaired scRNA-seq \+ scATAC-seq (multiome preferred), sufficient cell numbers (thousands), good coverage of TFs and accessible regions, and species-matched motif databases (human/mouse/fly supported). Sparse or low-quality data reduces accuracy.

Benchmarks in the original paper[5](https://paperpile.com/c/T1DyUg/qIr4) on ENCODE cell lines, PBMCs, melanoma states, and Drosophila data show that SCENIC+ recovers more relevant TFs, separates cell states better (higher ARI), predicts larger/high-quality regulons (e.g., 471 genes/1,152 regions per eRegulon vs. fewer/sparser in peers), and outperforms other methods in perturbation agreement and enhancer activity (STARR-seq). No major independent benchmarks of SCENIC+ exist beyond its own comparisons; however, reviews note persistent low overall GRN accuracy across tools [6](https://paperpile.com/c/T1DyUg/qIr4+5ONd), with SCENIC+ often cited as strong for enhancer-driven inference.

# Preparation & Pre-processing

A few essential housekeeping steps are necessary to get the data before culminating in gene regulatory network inference. First, a fresh computational environment is spun up—typically via Conda or Snakemake—where dependencies like pycisTopic, pycisTarget, and Snakemake are installed to ensure a smooth, reproducible run. Next, raw single-cell data are loaded and wrangled, with barcodes aligned and low-quality cells filtered out to keep signal amongst the noise.carve a clean dataset from the chaos. Finally, counts are normalized to iron out technical noise, and scaling is done to make sure features are on similar distributions and thus comparable.

Python Env Requirements

* Conda or Snakemake (recommended)  
* Python, for Snakemake build, remove “\<=3.11.8” from generic pyproject.toml  
* Run all “Snakemake …” commands from folder where snakefile exists

Input Data Requirements

To run the SCENIC+ workflow, the following specific inputs are required:

* cisTopic Object (.pkl): A pre-computed object containing topic modeling results from scATAC-seq data.  
* Gene Expression Data (.h5ad): An AnnData object containing scRNA-seq counts (raw or normalized).  
* Region Sets: A folder containing the genomic coordinates of regulatory regions (peaks) derived from scATAC-seq.  
* Motif Databases:  
* Rankings Database (.feather): For cisTarget (e.g., hg38\_screen\_v10\_rankings.feather).  
* Scores Database (.feather): For DEM (e.g., hg38\_screen\_v10\_clust...scores.feather).  
* Motif Annotations (.tbl): A table mapping motif IDs to Transcription Factors.  
* Genome Annotations: Reference genome files (e.g., genome\_annotation.tsv, chromsizes.tsv) matching the species (e.g., hg38).

**create new environment & install dependencies**

| git clone [https://github.com/aertslab/scenicplus](https://github.com/aertslab/scenicplus).git cd scenicplus git checkout development uv pin python 3.12 uv install . git clone https://github.com/aertslab/pycisTopic.gitcd pycisTopicpip install .wget https://cf.10xgenomics.com/samples/cell-arc/2.0.0/pbmc\_granulocyte\_sorted\_3k/pbmc\_granulocyte\_sorted\_3k\_filtered\_feature\_bc\_matrix.tar.gz *\#\# from https://www.10xgenomics.com/datasets/pbmc-from-a-healthy-donor-granulocytes-removed-through-cell-sorting-3-k-1-standard-2-0-0*tar \-xzf pbmc\_granulocyte\_sorted\_3k\_filtered\_feature\_bc\_matrix.tar.gz |
| :---- |

**load and process data**

| % python import scanpy as scimport pandas as pdadata \= sc.read\_10x\_mtx("filtered\_feature\_bc\_matrix/", var\_names="gene\_symbols")adata.var\["mt"\] \= adata.var\_names.str.startswith("MT-")sc.pp.calculate\_qc\_metrics(    adata, qc\_vars=\["mt"\], percent\_top=None, log1p=False, inplace=True) |
| :---- |

**normalize and scale data**

| adata.raw \= adatasc.pp.normalize\_total(adata, target\_sum=1e4)sc.pp.log1p(adata)sc.pp.highly\_variable\_genes(adata, min\_mean=0.0125, max\_mean=3, min\_disp=0.5)adata \= adata\[:, adata.var.highly\_variable\]sc.pp.scale(adata, max\_value=10)adata.write('GEX\_anndata\_PBMC.h5ad') |
| :---- |

In this case study we will use the multi-omics dataset provided by 10X genomics, which integrates gene expression and chromatin accessibility profiles from PBMC samples, stored as an AnnData object with dimensions of 2,711 observations (cells) by 36601 variables (features). The object includes comprehensive metadata for downstream analysis, detailed in Table 1\.

| Table1. Input Object Data and Metadata |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Observation-level annotations (obs) |  |  |
|  | cell-type labels  | e.g., VSN\_cell\_type, 'Seurat\_cell\_type |
|  | clustering results from multiple resolutions | e.g., VSN\_leiden\_res0.3 to 'VSN\_leiden\_res1.2, Seurat\_leiden\_res0.6 to Seurat\_leiden\_res1.2 |
|  | quality metrics/gene counts per cell | n\_genes\_by\_counts |
|  | /total UMI counts | total\_counts |
|  | /mitochondrial content | total\_counts\_mt, pct\_counts\_mt) |
|  | /sample identifiers  | VSN\_sample\_id |
| Variable-level annotations (var)  |  |  |
|  | gene identifiers | gene\_ids |
|  | feature types  | feature\_types  |
|  | mitochondrial gene flags | mt |
|  | statistical summaries/ mean expression | mean\_counts |
|  | /dropout rates | pct\_dropout\_by\_counts |
|  | /highly variable gene indicators  | highly\_variable, with associated means, dispersions, dispersions\_norm  |
| unstructured data (uns) |  |  |
|  | preprocessing artifacts/ highly variable  | hvg |
|  | /gene log-transformation metadata  | (log1p) |

Preprocessing steps leveraged these annotations to ensure data quality. We filtered cells based on *n\_genes\_by\_counts* and *pct\_counts\_mt* to remove low-quality profiles (e.g., those with excessive mitochondrial content), retaining a robust subset for analysis. Features were refined using *highly\_variable* flags to focus on biologically informative genes, aligning with standard single-cell practices. This curated dataset served as the foundation for subsequent gene regulatory network inference in SCENIC+.

# Inferring GRNs with SCENIC+ : breaking down the snakemake container

The SCENIC+ framework uses Snakemake to orchestrate a multi-step pipeline that integrates gene expression and chromatin accessibility data. This section details the key stages, from data preparation to network construction.

1\. Data Preparation (prepare\_GEX\_ACC)

The pipeline begins by integrating gene expression (GEX) and chromatin accessibility (ACC) data into a unified MuData object. This step is critical for ensuring that both modalities are aligned and ready for downstream analysis. Depending on the input (multiome or separate datasets), it merges modalities or generates pseudo-multiome data. Key steps include:

* Barcode Standardization: Ensuring cell barcodes match across both assays.  
* Quality Control: Filtering low-quality cells based on mitochondrial content and fragment counts.  
* Normalization: Normalizing gene expression counts and chromatin accessibility features to account for sequencing depth and technical variability.


snakemake \--forcerun prepare\_GEX\_ACC cistopic\_obj.pkl

OR

| uv run scenicplus prepare\_data prepare\_GEX\_ACC \\               \--cisTopic\_obj\_fname cistopic\_obj.pkl \\               \--GEX\_anndata\_fname GEX\_anndata\_PBMC.h5ad |
| :---- |

For the 10x PBMC dataset, this step processes the input to return a MuData object with 2,711 cells and \~470k features. It aligns cell barcodes, filters away low-quality cells (based on mitochondrial content and fragment counts), and retains comprehensive metadata for both modalities.

2\. Motif Enrichment (motif\_enrichment\_cistarget & motif\_enrichment\_dem)

SCENIC+ maps chromatin accessibility to TF activity using two complementary methods to identify potential regulatory interactions. These methods are run in parallel and results are merged downstream in \`prepare\_menr\`, the aggregate of which define \`cistromes\`.

* cisTarget: Uses Cluster-Buster to identify motif clusters in consensus peaks, prioritizing biological realism. It outputs enrichment scores (NES, AUC) to rank TFs based on their global enrichment across the dataset (Table 2).This evidence alone results in "direct" cistromes.  
* DEM (Differential Enrichment of Motifs): Compares foreground regions (e.g., cell-type specific peaks) to a background set to identify differentially enriched motifs. This is particularly useful for finding cell-type-specific drivers that might be missed by global enrichment methods (Figure 6). This evidence expands upon the direct, distal evidence, is lower-confidence and labeled "extended".

snakemake \--forcerun motif\_enrichment\_cistarget

OR

| uv run scenicplus grn\_inference motif\_enrichment\_cistarget \\               \--region\_set\_folder {input.region\_set\_folder} \\               \--cistarget\_db\_fname {input.ctx\_db\_fname} |
| :---- |

These steps generate tables detailing motif enrichment metrics (NES, AUC) and differential enrichment statistics (Log2FC, p-values), linking TFs to regulatory elements. This forms the basis for the "cistromes" used in later steps.

3\. eRegulon Data Preparation (prepare\_menr)

This step aggregates motif enrichment results and regulatory link predictions to define candidate eRegulons. It acts as a bridge between the motif discovery phase and the network inference phase. Specifically, it:

* Matches Motifs to TFs: Uses the motif annotation database to link enriched motifs to specific transcription factors.  
* Generates Cistromes: Creates collections of target regions for each TF. These are classified as "direct" (high-confidence, proximal) or "extended" (lower-confidence, distal) based on the strength of the motif evidence and genomic location.

snakemake \--forcerun prepare\_menr

OR

| uv run scenicplus prepare\_data prepare\_menr \\           \--paths\_to\_motif\_enrichment\_results {input.dem\_result\_fname} {input.ctx\_result\_fname} \\           \--multiome\_mudata\_fname {input.multiome\_mudata\_fname} |
| :---- |

4\. TF-to-Gene and Region-to-Gene Inference

Regulatory interactions are inferred via two parallel tracks to capture different aspects of regulation:

* TF-to-Gene: Predicts gene expression from TF expression using Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM). This step assesses the influence of each TF on potential target genes based on co-expression patterns (Figure 2B).  
* Region-to-Gene: Correlates chromatin accessibility at specific regions with nearby gene expression using Spearman Rank correlation. This identifies cis-regulatory links, determining which accessible regions are likely regulating which genes (Table 3).

snakemake \--forcerun tf\_to\_gene & region\_to\_gene

OR

| uv scenicplus grn\_inference TF\_to\_gene \\           \--multiome\_mudata\_fname {input.multiome\_mudata\_fname} \\uv scenicplus grn\_inference region\_to\_gene \\           \--multiome\_mudata\_fname {input.multiome\_mudata\_fname} \\           \--search\_space\_fname {input.search\_space} \\ |
| :---- |

5\. eRegulon Construction (eGRN\_direct & eGRN\_extended)

Finally, the pipeline synthesizes the outputs from the previous steps—TF-to-gene links, region-to-gene links, and motif enrichment—to construct the final eRegulons. Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) is used to identify the "leading-edge" target genes, ensuring that the regulons are biologically coherent.

* Direct eGRN: Captures immediate TF-to-gene relationships supported by high-confidence motif matches in proximal regulatory regions. These represent the strongest regulatory evidence (Figure 3).  
* Extended eGRN: Incorporates indirect or distal interactions, including lower-affinity motif matches or distal enhancers. This can broaden the regulatory landscape to capture more subtle or complex interactions (Figure 4).

snakemake \--forcerun eGRN\_direct & eGRN\_extended

OR

| uv scenicplus grn\_inference eGRN \\           \--TF\_to\_gene\_adj\_fname {input.tf\_to\_gene\_adjacencies}\\           \--region\_to\_gene\_adj\_fname {input.region\_to\_gene\_adjacencies} \\           \--cistromes\_fname {input.cistromes\_direct}\\           \--ranking\_db\_fname {input.ranking\_db\_fname} \\           \--eRegulon\_out\_fname {output.eRegulons\_direct}uv scenicplus grn\_inference eGRN \\           \--is\_extended \\           \--TF\_to\_gene\_adj\_fname {input.tf\_to\_gene\_adjacencies}\\           \--region\_to\_gene\_adj\_fname {input.region\_to\_gene\_adjacencies} \\           \--cistromes\_fname {input.cistromes\_extended} \\           \--ranking\_db\_fname {input.ranking\_db\_fname} \\           \--eRegulon\_out\_fname {output.eRegulons\_extended} |
| :---- |

The output (Tables 2 & 3\) are a comprehensive table of eRegulons, detailing TF-Region-Gene triplets and their associated importance scores. This table serves as the primary resource for downstream analysis, such as visualizing network topology or assessing regulon activity in specific cell types.

# 

# References

1\.	[Kamimoto, K. *et al.* Dissecting cell identity via network inference and in silico gene perturbation. *Nature* **614**, 742–751 (2023).](http://paperpile.com/b/T1DyUg/AeY9)  
2\.	[Fleck, J. S. *et al.* Inferring and perturbing cell fate regulomes in human brain organoids. *Nature* **621**, 365–372 (2023).](http://paperpile.com/b/T1DyUg/DKVp)  
3\.	[Kartha, V. K. *et al.* Functional inference of gene regulation using single-cell multi-omics. *Cell Genom* **2**, (2022).](http://paperpile.com/b/T1DyUg/AKsR)  
4\.	[Kamal, A. *et al.* GRaNIE and GRaNPA: inference and evaluation of enhancer-mediated gene regulatory networks. *Mol Syst Biol* **19**, e11627 (2023).](http://paperpile.com/b/T1DyUg/KnkS)  
5\.	[Bravo González-Blas, C. *et al.* SCENIC+: single-cell multiomic inference of enhancers and gene regulatory networks. *Nat Methods* **20**, 1355–1367 (2023).](http://paperpile.com/b/T1DyUg/qIr4)  
6\.	[Badia-I-Mompel, P. *et al.* Gene regulatory network inference in the era of single-cell multi-omics. *Nat Rev Genet* **24**, 739–754 (2023).](http://paperpile.com/b/T1DyUg/5ONd)

![][image1]

**Figure 1\.** SCENIC+ workflow. Topics and DARs inferred with pycisTopic are transformed into cistromes of directly bound regions by identifying modules that present significant enrichment of the regulator’s binding motif using pycisTarget. SCENIC+ integrates region accessibility, TF and target gene expression and cistromes to infer eGRNs, in which TFs are linked to their target regions and these to their target genes. Reproduced from original publication.

![][image2]

**Figure 2:** End-to-End Pipeline Flow. This diagram shows the full sequence of computational steps, their inputs/outputs, and how the Snakemake rules orchestrate the workflow.

**Figure 3:** eRegulon Logic How TF→ Region→ Gene Triplets Are Assembled. Diagramed is the core integration logic of SCENIC+, showing exactly how evidence from the three inference tracks (motif enrichment, TF-to-gene, and region-to-gene) is combined to produce the final eRegulon triplets.

![][image3]Figure 2\. Network Visualization of the inferred Gene Regulatory Network. (A) Nodes represent TFs and target genes, while edges represent regulatory interactions. Diagnostic plots showing the distribution of Node Degree (number of connections per node), Importance scores (from GBM), and Rho (correlation coefficients) from the SCENIC+ TF-to-gene output. (B) Directionality arrows can be seen in a zoomed-in preview, and are dynamically hidden natively in the tool when zoomed out. These metrics help in filtering for high-confidence interactions, here showing results when filtering for ρ\>0.8 (returning 802 nodes and 2749 edges). Node size conveys degree, color the bin of the degree as displayed in topmost histogram (C). Learn more about this plot at its link [https://l1nq.com/rmz1e9u](https://l1nq.com/rmz1e9u) 

| ![][image4] Figure 3\. Top 20 TFs by Regulon Size (Direct). This bar chart ranks transcription factors based on the number of target genes in their "direct" regulons, highlighting the most influential regulators in the dataset. | ![][image5] Figure 4\. Regulon Size Comparison: Direct vs Extended. A scatter plot comparing the number of target genes in direct versus extended regulons for each TF, illustrating the expansion of the regulatory network when including distal interactions. |
| :---- | :---- |

![][image6]  
Figure 5\. Distribution of Region-to-Gene Importance Scores. This histogram displays the distribution of importance scores assigned to region-gene links, providing insight into the overall strength of cis-regulatory associations detected in the dataset. The distribution reveals that region-to-gene importance scores are skewed, with many weak links and fewer strong ones, indicating not all accessible regions strongly regulate genes. It also shows that the highest scores aren't always for the closest regions, highlighting the role of distant enhancers in gene regulation.

![][image7]

Table 2\. CTX Results. Visualization of motif enrichment results from cisTarget, showing the top enriched motifs and their corresponding Normalized Enrichment Scores (NES), identifying key TFs associated with accessible chromatin regions. 

* NES \- Normalized Enrichment Score (higher \= more enriched)  
* AUC \- Area Under the Curve for recovery  
* Rank at max \- Rank at which max enrichment occurs

![][image8]  
Table 3\. DEM Results. Visualization of Differential Enrichment of Motifs (DEM) results, highlighting motifs that are significantly differentially enriched between foreground and background sets, pointing to cell-type-specific regulatory drivers.

* Log2 fold change of motif score (foreground vs background)  
* Adjusted p-value — BH-corrected significance  
* Mean foreground score — Average motif score in foreground regions  
* Mean background score — Average motif score in background regions
